cross_connections.display_label: was her mother-in-law; Sister of cotton-gin inventor Eli Whitney

narrative_blocks.header: Lost two children to disease in early 1836
narrative_blocks.body: In early 1836 Eliza Maria buried two of her young children within eight weeks — six-year-old Robert Pierrepont (HD1569) on January 17 and two-year-old Eliza Maria (HD1571) on March 13. She gave the name Eliza Maria again to a daughter born five years later (HD1576), passing down the name from the daughter she had lost.

cross_connections.display_label: was her mother-in-law; Sister of cotton-gin inventor Eli Whitney

narrative_blocks.header: Lost two children to disease in early 1836
narrative_blocks.body: In early 1836 Eliza Maria buried two of her young children within eight weeks — six-year-old Robert Pierrepont (HD1569) on January 17 and two-year-old Eliza Maria (HD1571) on March 13. She gave the name Eliza Maria again to a daughter born five years later (HD1576), passing down the name from the daughter she had lost.

orbit_connections.name: Gov. Henry Baldwin Harrison (via Frances Louisa Blake Osborn)
orbit_connections.relationship: Arthur Dimon Osborn — son of US Representative Thomas Burr Osborne, and husband of HD1572 Frances Louisa Blake — had a sister Elizabeth who married Governor Henry Baldwin Harrison of Connecticut
orbit_connections.notes: Frances Louisa Blake (HD1572) married Arthur Dimon Osborn. Arthur's father was US Representative Thomas Burr Osborne (CT). Arthur's sister Elizabeth married Henry Baldwin Harrison, Governor of Connecticut 1885-1887. Their son Thomas Burr Osborn (chemist) and Arthur Sherwood Osborn were grandchildren of Eli Whitney Blake. (Wikipedia on Eli Whitney Blake)

notable.notable_blurb: Blake stone crusher inventor, nephew of Eli Whitney

narrative_blocks.header: Patented the Blake stone crusher for road-building
narrative_blocks.detail: Blake graduated Yale in 1816 and trained at Litchfield Law School before abandoning law to work at his uncle Eli Whitney's armory in Whitneyville. He also held the first US mortise lock patent (1833, Blake Brothers) and served as president of the Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences. His son Henry Taylor Blake (HD1568) went on to build New Haven's park system — the stone-crusher's roads and the park commissioner's parks both serving the same expanding American city. (Wikipedia; research_notes)
narrative_blocks.body: Eli Whitney Blake patented his stone crusher in 1857. The first commercially successful machine of its kind, it made macadam roads practical on a national scale and is credited as foundational to American infrastructure. He is in the National Inventors Hall of Fame.

narrative_blocks.header: He left law for his uncle’s armory
narrative_blocks.body: Litchfield-trained, Blake abandoned the law for Eli Whitney’s armory; his 1833 mortise lock was the first such American patent, and his 1857 stone crusher put him in the National Inventors Hall of Fame. He died at ninety-one.

documents.title: 1830 letter from George Washington Blake (Boston) to Elihu Blake Jr. (NYC) mentioning Eli Whitney Blake
documents.description: Stampless letter from G.W. Blake (1803-?, brother) in Boston to Elihu Blake Jr. (1793-1875, brother) at No. 5 Park Place, NYC. Mentions Eli Whitney Blake by name in connection with Mr. W. prescribing 'be married before the first day of next August' for his malady. Also mentions parents Pa (Elihu Blake Sr.) enjoys tolerable health and mother (Elizabeth Fay Whitney) is well; siblings Maria, Elizabeth, Frances, Josiah Whitney, John Adams; uncle Blake; Aunt Whitney in New Haven; the September 1830 boiler explosion on the steamboat United States. Authors not built in JSON per Jalapeño Rule but documented here for family-correspondence record.

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied law there before abandoning law for the Whitney Armory.

career.organization: Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences

naming_inspiration.notes: Named "Pierrepont" honoring the Pierpont branch through mother Mary Elizabeth Blake's ancestry (H00388 → HD1556 → HD1565).

cross_connections.display_label: Published The Yale Gallinipper together 1846-1858 — three women writing anonymously as Yale undergraduates.
was his father-in-law; Namesake of Stiles Hall at UC Berkeley
was his mother-in-law; Donated original Stiles Hall building

narrative_blocks.header: Whitneyville to Berkeley: Gold Rush assayer father
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Whitneyville, Connecticut — the company town built around his family's Whitney Armory — Charles went west during the Gold Rush and assayed gold for Wells Fargo & Co. in San Francisco. He died in Berkeley in 1897, father of three notable children: Anson Stiles Blake, Eliza Seely Blake Thacher, and Robert Pierpont Blake.

narrative_blocks.header: Three children dead before age three, 1869-1883
narrative_blocks.body: Charles and Harriet Blake lost three of their seven children to early death across fourteen years: firstborn Jane in 1869 at eight days, Walter Whitney in 1879 at age two, and Henrietta MacWhorter in 1883 at eighteen months. Their four surviving children — Anson Stiles, Eliza Seely, Edwin Tyler, and Robert Pierpont — all reached adulthood.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and dead within eight days, August 1869
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Blake, firstborn of Charles Thompson Blake and Harriet Waters Stiles, was born in San Francisco on August 10, 1869 and died eight days later on August 18. She was the first of three Blake children to die young; her surviving four siblings were born across the next seventeen years.

notable.notable_blurb: Grand old man of Stiles Hall, UC Berkeley

education.notes: One of UC Berkeley's oldest living graduates at his death in 1959. Awarded honorary Doctor of Laws by the university.

career.organization: Blake Brothers Co. (Richmond, California — quarry firm)
University YMCA at UC Berkeley (Stiles Hall)
career.notes: Served as chairman for fifty years; declared 'Grand old man of Stiles Hall' by California State Legislature resolution.
Reactivated the Society in 1922 after it had lapsed; made honorary member for life.

career.organization: Save the Redwoods League / California Academy of Sciences / Friends of the Bancroft Library

cross_connections.display_label: also a pioneering historian of Gold Rush California

narrative_blocks.header: Grand old man of Stiles Hall, fifty years
narrative_blocks.body: Anson Stiles Blake chaired the board of the University YMCA at UC Berkeley for fifty years — the building, Stiles Hall, took its name from a benefactor in his Stiles family line. The California State Legislature formally named him the 'Grand old man of Stiles Hall' in recognition of that service.

narrative_blocks.header: Reactivated the California Historical Society, 1922
narrative_blocks.body: When the California Historical Society had lapsed, Anson Stiles Blake organized its 1922 revival and served as president. The Society later made him an honorary member for life. He sat on the boards of the Save the Redwoods League, California Academy of Sciences, and Friends of the Bancroft Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Family quarry firm at Richmond, California
narrative_blocks.body: Anson presided over Blake Brothers Co., the family quarry business at Richmond, California — extending into industrial materials a Blake-family talent for working stone that had begun with his grandfather Eli Whitney Blake's invention of the stone crusher in the 1850s.

burial.plot_notes: Memorial shared with husband Sherman Day Thacher.

education.notes: Graduated with A.B. degree from the state university in San Francisco/Berkeley in 1895.

institutions.notes: Raised six surviving children on the Thacher School campus from 1896.

narrative_blocks.header: Three infants lost in three years, 1898-1901
narrative_blocks.body: Between her firstborn Elizabeth (1897) and her son George (1903), Eliza Seely Blake Thacher lost three children in infancy: two unnamed sons in 1898 and 1900, and Charles Thacher in 1901. After this four-year cluster of grief she went on to bear six more children, all of whom survived to adulthood — among them Anson Stiles Thacher, who would later become headmaster of his father's Thacher School.

narrative_blocks.header: Walter died young
narrative_blocks.body: Walter died in 1879 at the age of two.

narrative_blocks.header: Henrietta died young
narrative_blocks.body: Henrietta died in 1883 at the age of one.

notable.notable_blurb: American Byzantinist; scholar of Armenian and Georgian cultures

education.notes: Fellowship years primarily spent in Russia studying Russian, Arabic, Syriac, Armenian, and Georgian languages.

career.notes: Helped found Tbilisi State University in 1918 and taught Greek language and Byzantine history there through 1920.
Instrumental in promoting Byzantine studies in the United States. Contributed to the study of medieval Georgian manuscripts found at Palestine and Mount Athos.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived the Russian Revolution at Tbilisi, 1918
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Pierpont Blake was already in Russia on a Harvard fellowship when the Revolution broke; he relocated to Tbilisi in 1918 on behalf of Saint Petersburg State University to investigate Tbilisi's manuscripts and the texts of the Bible. When Tbilisi State University was founded that same year he became one of its first professors, teaching Greek and Byzantine history through 1920.

narrative_blocks.header: Mount Athos, Palestine, and the Georgian manuscripts
narrative_blocks.body: Robert was instrumental in establishing Byzantine studies in the United States from his Harvard professorship beginning in 1920. He revealed and catalogued many medieval Georgian manuscripts at Palestine and Mount Athos, becoming a foundational figure in modern Byzantine and Caucasian-Christian scholarship.

narrative_blocks.header: Married in Tiflis as Soviet power consolidated, 1920
narrative_blocks.body: On January 7, 1920 Robert married Nadezhda Nikolavena Kryzhanovskaya in Tiflis, Georgia — months before the Soviets absorbed independent Georgia. The couple soon left for Harvard, where Robert would teach for thirty years.

notable.notable_blurb: Father of New Haven's park system, historian

narrative_blocks.header: Father of New Haven's park system
narrative_blocks.detail: "As far back as 1887 he foresaw the need of a modern and extended park system for the city," wrote Rev. Oscar Maurer at Blake's 1922 memorial. "Twenty-five years ago he was known as the Father of the New Haven park system, and it really was an appellation that belonged to him." He watched the boys and girls at Beaver Ponds and mapped it out for public use; he insisted on the expansion of East Rock; he foresaw Fort Hale as a park. He was elected honorary life member of the New Haven Chamber of Commerce in 1913 in recognition of this work. (HathiTrust Memorial Address 1922)
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed to the Commission on Public Parks in 1889, Blake served as president from 1898 to 1911. Nine parks totaling 650+ acres opened during his tenure. East Rock Park was expanded at his insistence; after his death, a portion was named Blake Field in his honor.

narrative_blocks.header: 'Chronicles of New Haven Green' (1898)
narrative_blocks.detail: Rev. Maurer described Blake's writing as "marked by accuracy, well-balanced judgment, wit of a truly Attic quality, and a whimsical humor which smiles at one from every page." Blake closed the Chronicles with a challenge still quoted in New Haven: "May it be guarded from encroachment in the future more jealously than in the past; and may our successors in its care of every race and lineage protect its soil, and cherish its traditions with that affectionate veneration which is the heritage and the test of every true son and daughter of New Haven." (Blake, Chronicles of New Haven Green, 1898)
narrative_blocks.body: Blake's 1898 history of the Green, drawn from papers read before the New Haven Colony Historical Society, became the definitive account of the city's public square from 1638 to 1862. He served as the Society's secretary for twenty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1848 — believed the oldest living Yale
narrative_blocks.detail: He was clerk of the Supreme and Superior Courts for Fairfield County (1868–1884), gave the principal address at New Haven's 250th anniversary in 1888, helped select and erect the Defenders' Monument, and was on Abraham Lincoln's Bridgeport reception committee in 1860. His mother was a great-granddaughter of Rev. James Pierpont (Yale co-founder), making his Yale connection an inheritance as well as an achievement. (HathiTrust Memorial Address 1922; Yale Biographical Sketches)
narrative_blocks.body: Blake graduated Yale in 1848 and was admitted to the bar in 1850. He practiced in Bridgeport as Treat & Blake until 1885, then returned to New Haven. He died April 6, 1922 — eleven days before his 94th birthday — believed to be the oldest Yale alumnus in New England.

narrative_blocks.header: 'The Rise and Fall' (1866)
narrative_blocks.detail: In later visits, Maurer found Blake "alert on the subject and keen to know what further contributions modern scholarship has made. He spoke modestly of his work — as always almost deprecatingly — said that it was a side issue, something done to satisfy his own mind on the subject." The same restless intelligence drove his park advocacy, his historical writing, and his long legal career. (HathiTrust Memorial Address 1922)
narrative_blocks.body: In 1866 Blake published The Rise and Fall (Hurd & Houghton), anticipating modern interpretations of the opening chapters of Genesis — years before the debate became mainstream in American churches. Rev. Maurer called it "certainly the first to be written by a layman" on the subject in America.

naming_inspiration.notes: Named to honor the Pierpont branch through mother Eliza Maria O'Brien Blake (HD1556).

narrative_blocks.header: Died one day before his fiftieth birthday, unmarried
narrative_blocks.body: George Augustus Blake was born January 2, 1832 in New Haven and died there January 1, 1882 — one day before his fiftieth birthday. He never married, the only one of Eliza Maria O'Brien Blake's twelve children to live to middle age without spouse or descendants documented here.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Arthur Osborne

notable.notable_blurb: Co-discovered Vitamin A, 1913; founded amino acid science

career.notes: Spent entire career here. Researches on vegetable proteins beginning 1888. Published 'The Vegetable Proteins' (1909). With Lafayette Mendel co-discovered Vitamin A in 1913, established essential amino acids (lysine and tryptophan) in healthy diet.

cross_connections.display_label: Yale science PhDs through different Blake siblings
cross_connections.notes: HD4112 (Yale PhD chemistry 1885) and HD4101 (Yale PhD math/physics 1925) — both descend from Eli Whitney Blake (X00503) and Eliza Maria O'Brien Blake (HD1556) through different children. The Blake-Whitney inventive tradition expressed through two generations of Yale scientists.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-discovered Vitamin A; founded amino acid science
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Burr Osborne and Lafayette Mendel — colleagues at the Connecticut Agricultural Experiment Station — independently co-discovered Vitamin A in 1913, the same year that Elmer McCollum and Marguerite Davis published the same finding three weeks earlier. Both papers appeared in the same issue of the Journal of Biological Chemistry; both teams established that vitamin deficiency caused xerophthalmia, opening modern nutritional biochemistry.

narrative_blocks.header: Isolated essential amino acids in rat nutrition studies
narrative_blocks.body: Working at the Connecticut Agricultural Experiment Station with 200 carefully fed rats, Osborne and Mendel showed which amino acids — lysine and tryptophan among them — are essential in a healthy diet. Casein became the first identified 'complete protein,' establishing nutrition science beyond caloric energy and toward structural amino acid requirements.

narrative_blocks.header: National Academy 1910, AAA&S 1914, APS 1921
narrative_blocks.body: Osborne was elected to the United States National Academy of Sciences in 1910, the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1914, and the American Philosophical Society in 1921 — the three foundational American scientific societies. He published over a hundred papers, most jointly with his collaborator Lafayette Mendel.

notable.notable_blurb: First Hazard Professor of Physics, Brown University (1870)

burial.plot_notes: Oak Dell Cemetery, South Kingstown RI (FA #2194924) — not yet built as CEM entry.

education.notes: Yale alumnus (precise year not documented in current source).

career.notes: Taught between 1866 and 1870 along with Columbia and Cornell.
One of three institutions where he taught 1866-1870.
One of three institutions where he taught 1866-1870.
First Hazard Professor of Physics — the inaugural holder of the named chair.

narrative_blocks.header: He held Brown's first physics chair
narrative_blocks.body: After teaching at Vermont, Columbia, and Cornell, Blake became the first Hazard Professor of Physics at Brown University in 1870. He held the chair until his death twenty-five years later.

narrative_blocks.header: His family made inventors and a stone-crusher
narrative_blocks.body: He was named for his great-uncle Eli Whitney, the cotton-gin inventor, and his father devised the Blake stone-breaker that paved roads. The elder Blake had also helped Samuel Morse in the early days of the telegraph.

death.cause: killed in action, Battle of Cedar Mountain

naming_inspiration.notes: Named "Pierrepont" honoring the Pierpont branch through mother Eliza Maria O'Brien Blake (HD1556).
Replacement naming: first child Eliza Maria Blake (HD1571) died in childhood 1836.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, Connecticut

H00388.prominent_name_reason: John Pierpont (1740-1805) — great-grandfather of J.P. Morgan (1837-1913). Chain: John Pierpont (H00388) → James Pierpont → John Pierpont the poet (1785-1866, abolitionist) → Juliet Pierpont × Junius Spencer Morgan → John Pierpont Morgan. J.P. Morgan's middle name "Pierpont" commemorates this descent from the Pierpont family of New Haven — founded by Rev. James Pierpont, who married Mary Hooker (H00034). Thomas Hooker's great-great-great-granddaughter's grandson became the most powerful financier in American history.

bio.notes: Sources also give surname as "Pierrepont" — alternate spelling

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandfather of J.P. Morgan
narrative_blocks.body: His great-grandson was John Pierpont Morgan (1837-1913), the financier whose name 'J.P. Morgan' carries forward this Pierpont line. Through his son James Pierpont and grandson John Pierpont the poet, the name traveled four generations to one of America's most consequential bankers.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived in old Pierpont mansion, New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Dwight described him as 'a large landholder who lived in the old family-mansion at New Haven.' He had ten children, of whom only four reached adulthood and only three married.

narrative_blocks.header: Family later restored 'Pierrepont' French spelling
narrative_blocks.body: He was born 'Pierpont' but late in his life, around 1802, Hezekiah Beers Pierpont requested the family restore the original French 'Pierrepont' spelling. Hezekiah did not adopt it himself for business reasons; descendants split between the two spellings thereafter.

narrative_blocks.header: She buried seven children and lived to ninety
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Beers married John Pierpont and bore ten children, but only four reached adulthood; she named three daughters Sarah and lost two as infants and the third at fourteen. She lived to ninety, outliving her husband by thirty years.

narrative_blocks.header: The British seized her home as a hospital
narrative_blocks.body: During the 1779 British invasion of New Haven, soldiers raided the house her husband John Pierpont had built and took it for use as a hospital and headquarters. The home stood at the center of the town's worst wartime days.

narrative_blocks.header: Her brother guarded the captured spy Major André
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah's brother, Captain Nathan Beers, commanded the guard over the British spy Major John André at his trial and execution in 1780. André's hanging, in the wake of Benedict Arnold's treason, was among the most talked-of events of the war.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
